source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
In a square with sides of length 6 the points $A$ and $B$ are on a line joining the midpoints of the opposite sides of the square (see the figure). When you draw lines from $A$ and $B$ to two opposite vertices, you divide the square in three parts of equal area. What is the length of $A B$?
<image1>

image_dependency: The setup depends on spatial relations inside a square: points A and B lie on a midpoint-joining line and are connected to opposite vertices
multi_step: Solving requires combining geometry of the square with equal-area constraints
The problem asks for a derived segment length AB rather than directly reading a value
Likely requires determining positions of A and B from area partition conditions before computing AB
verifiability: answer_type is numeric and answer_text is a single value ('4')
The geometry conditions are precise enough to support algebraic or area-based checking

risk_flags: diagram-specific interpretation of which opposite vertices are connected may matter

decision_reasons: Assets are complete: question, image, and numeric answer are all present, and registry passed with no reported issues
Initial scoring is strong for image dependency, multi-step reasoning, and verifiability
The diagram-specific interpretation risk is noted, but the required figure is available, so core semantics remain recoverable and support inclusion
